mnemonics.title: COPY Constructor Call Scenarios
mnemonics.content: P.A.R.R. - Pass by value, Assignment initialization, Return by value, Reference binding (actually NO copy)

mnemonics.title: Rule of Three
mnemonics.content: D.C.A. - Destructor, Copy constructor, Assignment - define all three if you define one

mnemonics.title: Deep vs Shallow Copy
mnemonics.content: D.S.C. - Deep means Separate memory allocation, Shallow means Common address/shared pointer

mnemonics.title: Parameter Requirements
mnemonics.content: C.O.N.S.T. - Const (protects source), Object reference (prevents infinite loop), No change to source, Same class type, Takes single argument

acronyms.usage: Abbreviation used in code comments and discussions
Compiler optimization that elides copy when returning local objects
RVO for named variables instead of temporary values

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box representing function call stack. When obj1 passes by value into function, draw an arrow creating new box obj1_copy invoking copy constructor. For initialization MyClass obj2 = obj1, show obj1 as template creating obj2.
Draw two circles for objects. For shallow: both point to same rectangle (shared heap memory). For deep: each has its own rectangle showing separate heap allocations.
Triangle with three corners: Destructor, Copy Constructor, Copy Assignment. Draw lines connecting all three - if you touch one, you must touch all three.

analogies.analogy: Think of copy constructor like a photocopier - it creates an exact duplicate of the original document. Just as photocopy gives you a separate paper with same content, deep copy gives you independent object with same data.

Shallow copy is like two students sharing one notebook - both see the same information. If one writes in it, the other sees changes. This is dangerous when both try to 'destroy' the notebook (double-free error).
Passing by reference is like showing someone the original photo - no new photo is made. Passing by value is like making a printout for them - a new copy is created using the copy constructor.
